citation: De Arruda et al. (2025) - Cost-benefit analysis of solar energy integration in buildings
description: Section-skeleton few-shot example for design strategy extraction. Yellow-highlighted passages from the annotated PDF are preserved as relevant_snippets.
section_skeleton:
Paper structure (De Arruda et al., 2025 — 14 pages, ~65,000 characters):
--- TITLE & ABSTRACT (page 1) ---
[...~2000 chars about cost-benefit analysis of solar energy in affordable housing, mentioning photovoltaic panels and Solar Heating Systems (SHS) — IGNORE THIS, it describes the study scope not implemented strategies]
--- INTRODUCTION (pages 1-3) ---
[...~8000 chars of background on global solar energy adoption, Brazilian energy matrix, literature review of PVsol/TRNSYS studies, Brazilian standards ABNT NBR 15569, 15575, 16690 — IGNORE THIS, these are literature review and policy context only]
--- METHODOLOGY (pages 3-5) ---
[...~5000 chars describing the three-model framework, mathematical model for SHS, equations for Vconsumption, Vstorage, Euseful, Acollector — IGNORE THIS, these are general methods not specific to the case study building]
--- CASE STUDY (pages 4-6) --- ← LOOK HERE
"To validate the proposed methodology, a case study is presented. The chosen subject is Brazilian affordable housing, considering a single house from the Brazilian federal government program called 'Minha Casa Minha Vida.'"
"For the case study, the Vale da Mata condominium was selected."
"In line with the neighborhood impact study, it is worth noting that the Vale da Mata condominium was initially equipped with a basic solar water heating system."
"These assessments revolve around incorporating photovoltaic energy and the thermal water heating system."
"Model 1: This model entails a grid-connected photovoltaic project with a zero energy balance objective. The system's design is tailored to generate the entirety of the energy consumed by the functional unit."
"Model 2: This model represents a photovoltaic project connected to the grid. It features two solar panels, each rated at 340 watts, and is calibrated with predefined loads amounting to 680 W."
"Model 3: This model is designed with a Solar Heating System (SHS)."
--- SIMULATION DETAILS (pages 7-9) --- ← ALSO LOOK HERE
"Model 1 adopts four modules from the manufacturer ZNShine PV-Tech Co., Ltd., each with a rating of 340W, characterized by the specification 'ZXP6-72-340.' These modules are positioned vertically."
"Configuring the inverters is the next step in the process, with an inverter capacity of 1.5 kWh selected from the manufacturer GROWATT."
"In Model 2, the configuration includes two modules from the manufacturer ZNShine PV-Tech Co., Ltd., each with a capacity of 340 W... a 750 W inverter from the manufacturer GROWATT is chosen."
"For Model 3, the solar heating system for the shower... a family of three people... The system design involves determining the volume of the hot water reservoir and the number of solar collectors (plates) required."
"Model 3 employs a 100-L boiler and two solar collectors with an AMSE yield of 81.30 kWh/(m2xmonth) and an area of 1.41 m2 each, totaling a solar absorption area of 2.82 m2."
--- RESULTS (pages 9-12) ---
[...~20000 chars of simulation results for Models 1, 2, 3, financial analysis tables, CO2 reduction comparisons — IGNORE THIS for strategy identification, these confirm outcomes not new strategies]
--- CONCLUSION (pages 12-14) ---
[...~5000 chars summarizing findings — IGNORE THIS]

relevant_snippets.text: the Vale da Mata condominium was initially equipped with a basic solar water heating system. Given this context, the condominium-type residence serves as an apt model for evaluating feasibility hypotheses. These assessments revolve around incorporating photovoltaic energy and the thermal water heating system.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Establishes that the baseline building had a solar water heating system and that the case study evaluates PV and thermal water heating strategies

relevant_snippets.text: Model 1: This model entails a grid-connected photovoltaic project with a zero energy balance objective. The system's design is tailored to generate the entirety of the energy consumed by the functional unit.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Defines the first design strategy: a grid-connected PV system sized for full energy self-sufficiency (zero energy balance)

relevant_snippets.text: Model 2: This model represents a photovoltaic project connected to the grid. It features two solar panels, each rated at 340 watts, and is calibrated with predefined loads amounting to 680 W.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Defines the second design strategy: a smaller grid-connected PV system with two fixed 340W panels

relevant_snippets.text: Model 3: This model is designed with a Solar Heating System (SHS).
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Defines the third design strategy: a solar thermal heating system for domestic hot water

relevant_snippets.text: Model 1 adopts four modules from the manufacturer ZNShine PV-Tech Co., Ltd., each with a rating of 340W, characterized by the specification 'ZXP6-72-340.' These modules are positioned vertically. The shading analysis is a crucial consideration; while no shading interference is identified in this scenario.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Provides specific hardware detail for Model 1: four 340W monocrystalline PV modules mounted vertically on the roof

relevant_snippets.text: an inverter capacity of 1.5 kWh selected from the manufacturer GROWATT for the string consisting of four photovoltaic modules. This particular inverter is designated as 'GROWAT New Energy Co., ltd. I Growat-1500-S.'
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Specifies the inverter component for Model 1's grid-connected PV system

relevant_snippets.text: In Model 2, the configuration includes two modules from the manufacturer ZNShine PV-Tech Co., Ltd., each with a capacity of 340 W... Subsequently, a shading analysis is conducted to optimize panel placement, reducing shading as much as possible while allowing space for a solar water heating system primarily intended for shower usage.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Confirms Model 2 PV configuration and notes space co-existence with solar thermal for shower — two strategies on one roof

relevant_snippets.text: For Model 3, the solar heating system for the shower, designed following the ABNT—NBR 15569 parameters, is dimensioned considering a residence accommodating a family of three people. The system design involves determining the volume of the hot water reservoir and the number of solar collectors (plates) required. The hot water is intended to be stored at 50°C and consumed at 38°C.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Provides detailed specification for Model 3 Solar Heating System: shower-focused, family-sized, thermal storage at 50°C

relevant_snippets.text: Model 3 employs a 100-L boiler and two solar collectors with an AMSE yield of 81.30 kWh/(m2xmonth) and an area of 1.41 m2 each, totaling a solar absorption area of 2.82 m2.
relevant_snippets.why_relevant: Gives hardware specifics for Model 3: 100-litre storage boiler and two solar thermal collectors totalling 2.82m²

design_strategies.name: Grid-connected photovoltaic system — zero energy balance (Model 1)
context: Model 1 entails a grid-connected photovoltaic project with a zero energy balance objective. The system's design is tailored to generate the entirety of the energy consumed by the functional unit.

Model 1 adopts four modules from the manufacturer ZNShine PV-Tech Co., Ltd., each with a rating of 340W, characterized by the specification 'ZXP6-72-340.' These modules are positioned vertically on the residence roof.

design_strategies.name: Grid-connected photovoltaic system — fixed 2-panel configuration (Model 2)
context: Model 2 represents a photovoltaic project connected to the grid. It features two solar panels, each rated at 340 watts, and is calibrated with predefined loads amounting to 680 W.
In Model 2, the configuration includes two modules from ZNShine PV-Tech Co., Ltd., each with a capacity of 340 W, and a 750 W inverter from the manufacturer GROWATT is chosen.

design_strategies.name: Solar Heating System for domestic hot water (Model 3)
context: For Model 3, the solar heating system for the shower is dimensioned considering a residence accommodating a family of three people. The hot water is intended to be stored at 50°C and consumed at 38°C. The annual average ambient temperature is 23.7°C.
Model 3 employs a 100-L boiler and two solar collectors with an AMSE yield of 81.30 kWh/(m2xmonth) and an area of 1.41 m2 each, totaling a solar absorption area of 2.82 m2.

design_strategies.name: Baseline solar water heating system (pre-existing)
context: the Vale da Mata condominium was initially equipped with a basic solar water heating system. Given this context, the condominium-type residence serves as an apt model for evaluating feasibility hypotheses around incorporating photovoltaic energy and the thermal water heating system.

lesson: This paper evaluates THREE distinct design strategy configurations (Models 1, 2, 3) on the same case study building. The model must extract ALL three, not just the first mentioned. Model specifications appear across TWO sections: the Case Study introduction (pages 4-6) names the strategies, and the Simulation sub-sections (pages 7-9) provide hardware details — both sections must be read. The methodology section (pages 3-5) contains general equations that apply to any building and must NOT be treated as case-study-specific strategies. A pre-existing baseline strategy (the original solar water heating system) is also worth capturing at lower confidence, as it provides important context but is not the focus of the analysis.
